title: The Voo Sound: Vibrating the Vagus Nerve Back to Safety
dek: *You've been told anger lives in your head, that you need to think your way out of it or talk it through. But anger is a full-body alarm, and the fastest route back to safety runs through a nerve you can hum into submission. Here's how a single sound can turn your internal siren off.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. The "voo" sound is a low-pitched humming technique that activates your vagus nerve and signals your nervous system to exit fight-or-flight. This vocal vagal tone practice reduces inflammation, lowers heart rate, and helps metabolize stored anger without verbal processing. In our experience, three minutes of vooing can shift you from rage to regulated presence when talking feels impossible.

You've been told that anger is something you manage with your mind. That's the first lie. Anger doesn't live in your prefrontal cortex, waiting politely for cognitive reframing. It lives in your brainstem, your gut, the clenched fist of your diaphragm. It's a autonomic response that floods your body before your thinking brain even gets the memo. And when you're stuck in that flood, when the sympathetic nervous system has hijacked every cell, talking rarely helps. Sometimes words make it worse, because language requires cortical processing you don't have access to when you're flooded. That's where the voo sound comes in. ## What the Voo Sound Actually Is The voo is a low-pitched, sustained hum that sounds like a foghorn made of breath. You're not singing. You're not chanting a mantra. You're making a deliberate vibration in the lowest comfortable register of your voice, letting it rumble through your chest, throat, and skull for as long as your exhale lasts. Then you breathe in quietly through your nose and do it again. The pitch matters. High-pitched sounds stimulate alertness. Low-pitched sounds, especially those that vibrate the bones of your face and the soft tissues of your throat, send a mechanical signal directly to the vagus nerve. That nerve is the primary channel of the parasympathetic nervous system, the rest-and-digest counterweight to fight-or-flight. When you activate it, you tell your body the threat has passed. In my own practice, I've watched clients go from red-faced, trembling rage to soft-eyed stillness in under five minutes of sustained vooing. No insight required. No narrative shift. Just vibration, and the body's willingness to believe what it feels. ## The Vagus Nerve and the Anger Alarm Your vagus nerve is the longest cranial nerve in your body, wandering from your brainstem down through your throat, heart, lungs, and digestive tract. It's a two-way information highway. About 80% of its fibers send signals *up* to the brain, reporting on the state of your internal organs. The other 20% send commands *down*, telling your heart to slow, your breathing to deepen, your gut to resume digestion. When you're angry, your sympathetic nervous system has already made a series of executive decisions without consulting you. It's diverted blood away from your digestive organs and into your limbs. It's spiked your cortisol and adrenaline. It's narrowed your peripheral vision and locked your jaw. The NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system documents how this cascade unfolds in milliseconds, bypassing conscious control entirely. The vagus nerve is your emergency brake. When you stimulate it, you interrupt the sympathetic loop. Your heart rate variability increases. Inflammation markers drop. The muscles around your eyes soften. You're not suppressing the anger; you're changing the physiological substrate it's riding on. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, has long emphasized that trauma and intense emotion are stored in the body as incomplete survival responses. You can't think your way out of a nervous system state that was never cognitive to begin with. You have to complete the cycle somatically. The voo sound is one of the fastest somatic completions I know. ## Why Humming Beats Talking When You're Flooded Soraya Chemaly writes in *Rage Becomes Her* that women, in particular, are socialized to verbalize and explain their anger before they've even felt it fully in their bodies. We're taught to make our anger legible, defensible, small enough to fit into words. But when you're flooded, the language centers of your brain are offline. The blood has literally left the building. Trying to articulate what you feel when you're in sympathetic overdrive is like trying to write a sonnet while sprinting. The voo sound requires no language. It asks nothing of your prefrontal cortex. You don't need to know *why* you're angry or whether your anger is justified. You just need to be willing to make a sound that vibrates the bones of your face. Over the years I've seen clients who've spent thousands of dollars in talk therapy, circling the same anger stories, getting more articulate about their rage without ever actually discharging it. The body doesn't care about your narrative. It cares about signals. And the signal the voo sends is unmistakable: *We are safe enough to exhale fully. We are safe enough to lower our guard.* ## The Five-Minute Voo Practice Here's how you do it. You don't need special equipment. You don't need silence. You can do this in your car, in a bathroom stall, standing in your kitchen with your back against the counter. Sit or stand comfortably. Place one hand on your chest, one on your belly. Inhale slowly through your nose, letting your belly rise. Then, on the exhale, begin a low-pitched "voo" sound, as deep as you can comfortably go without straining. Let it vibrate your sternum, your throat, the roof of your mouth. Sustain it for the length of your exhale. Pause. Breathe in quietly through your nose. Exhale on another voo. Repeat for three to five minutes. You'll know it's working when you feel a shift in your chest, a softening around your eyes, or a sudden yawn. Yawning is a vagal reset. So is sighing. Let them happen. If you start to cry, that's also discharge. Let it move. The practice isn't about controlling the anger. It's about giving your nervous system permission to stand down. ## What Happens in Your Body When You Voo The mechanics are straightforward. The vibration of the voo stimulates mechanoreceptors in the tissues surrounding the vagus nerve. Those receptors send a signal up to the brainstem: *low-frequency vibration detected, interpret as safety cue*. The brainstem responds by activating the ventral vagal complex, the branch of the parasympathetic system responsible for social engagement and calm. Your heart rate slows. Your blood pressure drops. The smooth muscles in your digestive tract resume peristalsis. The tiny muscles around your eyes, which had been locked in a vigilance pattern, begin to soften. You might notice your shoulders drop, or your jaw unclench. These aren't things you're doing; they're things your body is doing in response to the signal you've sent. Thich Nhat Hanh often spoke about the bell of mindfulness, the way a single sound can call you back to your body. The voo is that bell. It's not a distraction from the anger. It's a way of meeting the anger exactly where it lives, in the tissues and nerves, and offering it a different pathway. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the same principle: the body leads, the mind follows. You can't think your way into a parasympathetic state. But you can hum your way there. ## When to Voo and When to Move The voo is most effective when you're flooded but not actively in motion. If your anger is asking you to pace, to throw something, to slam a door, honor that impulse first. Anger often carries a movement signature. Let your body complete the movement, then voo. I've had clients who need to stomp their feet for two minutes, or punch a pillow, or shake their arms out like they're flinging off water, before the voo will land. That's fine. The vooing isn't a bypass. It's a bridge back. If you're someone whose anger runs hot and fast, you might pair the voo with a cooling practice afterward. [Tattva's Herbs Ayurvedic Pitta Cooling Massage Oil 8oz](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07Q3T2N6Z?tag=spankyspinola-20) on your temples and the soles of your feet can help extend the settling. If your anger is more of a slow burn, a smoldering resentment that never quite peaks, you might need to stoke it first. Let yourself feel the heat fully before you voo it down. The nervous system doesn't respond well to ambivalence. If you're going to discharge, discharge. If you're going to settle, settle. The voo is a settling tool. ## Vooing in Relationship Here's where it gets interesting. You can voo in the presence of another person, and it changes the field between you. When two nervous systems are in proximity, they influence each other. Mirror neurons fire. Heart rhythms begin to sync. If you're in an argument and both people are in sympathetic arousal, you're essentially two alarm systems screaming at each other, and no new information can get through. If one person drops into a voo, the other person's nervous system will often follow. Not always, and not immediately, but the shift is palpable. I've taught this to couples who were on the edge of divorce, who couldn't get through a single conversation without escalating. One partner would start to voo, right there in the middle of the kitchen, and the other would stop mid-sentence, confused, then curious, then quieter. You're not performing the voo *at* someone. You're regulating yourself in their presence, and regulation is contagious. If you're new to this work, you might want to explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) to get a clearer picture of where your nervous system tends to default. Knowing your baseline makes it easier to notice when you've left it. ## Supporting the Vagus Beyond the Voo The voo is a rescue tool, not a replacement for a broader nervous system care plan. If you're chronically stuck in sympathetic overdrive, if anger is your default frequency, you need more than a five-minute hum. You need sleep, omega-3s, cold exposure, social connection, and probably some trauma-informed bodywork. That said, there are a few things that support vagal tone in ways that compound with the voo. [Maitake Products, Mushroom Wisdom Super Lion's Mane - 120 vcaps](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000KHTT0A?tag=spankyspinola-20) has been shown to support neuroplasticity and reduce inflammation, both of which help the nervous system find new patterns. [Vital Nutrients Lithium Orotate 5mg - 90 Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z4ZQB4?tag=spankyspinola-20) in microdoses can stabilize mood and reduce the intensity of emotional swings, making it easier to catch yourself before you're fully flooded. Cold water on your face, gargling, singing, laughing, all of these stimulate the vagus. The voo isn't precious. It's one tool in a kit. Use it when it fits. If you're looking for a broader philosophy of emotional and spiritual practice, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers a companion perspective that weaves these nervous system principles into a larger framework of embodied divination and self-knowledge. You might also find [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) useful for understanding which plants support different nervous system states. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Can I voo too much or damage my voice? No. The voo is a gentle, low-pitched hum, not a forceful shout. You're not straining your vocal cords. If you feel any discomfort in your throat, you're pushing too hard or pitching too high. Drop the pitch lower, soften the volume, and let the vibration do the work. Some people voo for twenty minutes at a time without any issue. Listen to your body. ### What if I feel more agitated after I start vooing? Sometimes when you drop out of sympathetic arousal, you'll hit a pocket of grief or fear that was underneath the anger. That's not the voo making you worse; that's the voo revealing what was already there. If that happens, keep vooing, or switch to a different somatic tool like shaking or tapping. The agitation will pass if you stay with it. ### Is this the same as toning or chanting? Not quite. Toning often uses higher pitches and is oriented toward energetic or spiritual experiences. Chanting usually involves specific syllables or phrases with cultural or religious meaning. The voo is purely mechanical. You're stimulating a nerve to shift a physiological state. It works whether you believe in it or not. ## The Body's Oldest Language You don't need to understand the vagus nerve to benefit from the voo. Your body already knows what to do with a low, sustained vibration. It's been responding to that signal since before you had language, since before you had a self-concept. The hum of a mother's voice. The rumble of thunder receding. The purr of safety returning. When you voo, you're speaking the body's oldest language. You're saying, *I'm here, I'm listening, I'm willing to come back.* And the body, which has been waiting for that signal, softens. Not because the anger was wrong, but because the alarm has done its job. The threat, real or imagined, has been noted. Now it's time to reset. Now it's time to breathe. If you want to know more about how we approach these questions, about the philosophy and practice that underlies this work, [the about page](/about) offers a fuller picture. But you don't need a philosophy to start. You just need a breath, a willingness, and a sound you've been carrying in your chest your whole life, waiting to be used. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the voo sound: vibrating the vagus nerve back to safety, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
